coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent graphene quantum dots and its application in selective curcumin detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Tade, Rahul S.
Patil, Pravin O.
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we present a facile low-cost and eco-friendly approach for conversion of bamboo timber waste (Bf) derived cellulose nanocrystals (Bf-CNCs) into strong blue luminescent graphene quantum dots (Bf-GQDs) by hydrothermal route. The various properties of synthesized Bf-GQDs were investigated using different spectroscopic techniques. The probable mechanism of Bf-GQDs formation from Bf-CNCs and the effect of pH, particle size on the fluorescent properties of Bf-GQDs also executed. Furthermore, Bf-GQDs were used for the detection of curcumin in an aqueous environment which is the major prerequisite of the present study. The Bf-GQDs showed remarkable photoluminescence (PL) quenching kinetics toward the curcumin (LOD 30.0 nM L−1) assessed by Stern-Volmer plot. The practicability of the method assessed using ginger rhizome juice, while the selectivity of the Bf-GQDs evaluated against different metal ions and different biochemicals. The proposed method will support to establish the strategies for the detection of biochemicals from the aqueous system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bamboo timber waste
Cellulose nanocrystals
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)
Curcumin detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of synthesized Bf-CNCs and Bf-GQDs
Optimization and discretion of synthesis methods
Mechanism of GQDs formation from cellulose nanocrystals
Effect of pH, particle size, and zeta potential on the fluorescent properties of Bf-GQDs
Mechanism of Bf-GQDs quenching by curcumin
Practical application of curcumin sensing in real samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2020 Korean Physical Society. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic of Bf-GQDs formation from Bf-CNCs.
UV–vis spectrum of curcumin (a), Bf-GQDs (b), spectra at different pH buffers (c), Excitation dependent fluorescence of Bf-GQDs (d) and fluorescence at different concentrations (f).
Scanning electron microscopy and elemental analysis of Bf-CNCs (a, b), Bf-GQDs (c, d), and FT-IR of Bf-CNCs and Bf-GQDs (e).
High-resolution transmission electron microscopy of Bf-CNCs (a and b) and Bf-GQDs (c, d, and e) at different scales, SAED pattern of Bf-GQDs (f).

X-ray powder diffraction pattern of Bf-CNCs (a), Bf-GQDs (b), Raman spectra of Bf-GQDs (c), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy survey spectrum of Bf-GQDs (d), high-resolution XPS spectra of Bf-GQDs for Oxygen and carbon (e, f).

The fluorescence quenching responses of the Bf-GQDs after the addition of different concentrations of curcumin with quenching efficiency (A), plausible mechanisms behind fluorescence quenching of Bf-GQDs by curcumin (B) and FRET mechanism of Bf-GQDs based Quenching by curcumin (C).

Selectivity analysis of the Bf-GQDs against curcumin and metal ions (the concentration of all metal ions: 100 μM, (‘*’ and ‘#’ denotes the intensity of blank Bf-GQDs in absence of curcumin and after addition of curcumin, respectively).
Results of the curcumin recovery performed in ginger juice.
Comparison of the proposed and reported methods used for curcumin detection.

row.entry: Source and type of sensing material

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent graphene quantum dots and its application in selective curcumin detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur, 425405, Maharashtra, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur, 425405, Maharashtra, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Dept. of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur, 425405, Maharashtra, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we present a facile low-cost and eco-friendly approach for conversion of bamboo timber waste (Bf) derived cellulose nanocrystals (Bf-CNCs) into strong blue luminescent graphene quantum dots (Bf-GQDs) by hydrothermal route. The various properties of synthesized Bf-GQDs were investigated using different spectroscopic techniques. The probable mechanism of Bf-GQDs formation from Bf-CNCs and the effect of pH, particle size on the fluorescent properties of Bf-GQDs also executed. Furthermore, Bf-GQDs were used for the detection of curcumin in an aqueous environment which is the major prerequisite of the present study. The Bf-GQDs showed remarkable photoluminescence (PL) quenching kinetics toward the curcumin (LOD 30.0 nM L−1) assessed by Stern-Volmer plot. The practicability of the method assessed using ginger rhizome juice, while the selectivity of the Bf-GQDs evaluated against different metal ions and different biochemicals. The proposed method will support to establish the strategies for the detection of biochemicals from the aqueous system.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene based materials, especially graphene quantum dots (GQDs) accolades by researchers amongst the different nanomaterials due to its exceptional properties. Since a scalable GQDs production method still stands as a serious obstacle due to its broad application in numerous areas. For example, bottom-up synthesis from graphene and its native precursors demands costly and potentially hazardous chemicals. Moreover, these methods are tedious and required multiple steps to the obtained purified final product (GQDs), which ultimately results in low production yield. Several endeavors have been made worldwide for the conversion of natural waste precursors (NPs) such as weathered leaves [1], coffee grounds [2], etc. Into graphene-like materials and GQDs [3]. It was observed that GQDs from aforementioned precursors has the greatest biocompatibility and stability as compared to the GQDs from pristine graphene as they obtained from a series of hazardous chemical treatment. Moreover, these biomass-derived GQDs are superior to semiconductor quantum dots in terms of solubility, aqueous stability, chemical modification, and high resistance to photo bleaching [4]. These ideal properties have now been subjugated for numerous applications such as fluorescent probes for solar cells [5], biochemical sensing [6] or detection [7,8], bioimaging [9], etc. Apart from the use of available biomaterials such as citric acid [10], sucrose [11], for obtaining GQDs or graphitic materials one can use waste biomass for gaining one or more active materials of interest. For instance, corn agro waste can be converted into cellulosic derivatives [12], wood waste into quality graphene [13]. The research fraternity well realized that to achieve the ‘sustainable green synthesis goals’ it is necessary to look after the abundant waste biomass to be utilized for the synthesis of graphene-like materials [3,14,15]. It is well known that cellulose-based biomass hitting the top list as abundant waste at agricultural as well as domestic levels. Cellulose-based polymers had been utilized for the fabrication of numerous materials like nanocomposites in many biomedical and pharmaceutical applications, etc. [16]. A huge amount of bamboo timber waste produces at the various sawmills which further expelled as waste or used as domestic fuel. Bamboo fibers mostly used as a natural composite reinforcing material because of its high strength-to-weight ratio and high hydrophilicity. Cellulose is the major component of the bamboo fibers which plays a crucial role as reinforcing materials in composites while hemicelluloses after the efficient conversion accomplish biofuels and multifunctional by-products. In addition to this hemicellulose on hydrolytic undergoes cleavage of glycosidic bonds between two anhydrous glucose which is the most significant rout of cellulose degradation [17]. Inspired by these facts, we choose the bamboo timber waste for the GQDs synthesis. The stepwise processing of bamboo timber waste yields us cellulose nanocrystals as well as GQDs which seems to be the best utilization of this waste biomass.

Curcumin, an active herbal compound isolated using different non-polar solvents based extractions methods at the industries. However, its traces remain in menstruum, and this menstruum directly decanted in sewage water canals. It was evidenced that curcumin exerts immunomodulatory effects in some aquatic species [18]. Curcumin is practically insoluble in water, hence it has limited traceability in an aqueous system. At present, curcumin detection utilized numerous spectrophotometric and chromatographic methods. Though these experimental set-ups have successfully determined curcumin, most of these demands highly skilled personnel, costly equipment, complicated pre-treatment, and are time-consuming [19,20]. A huge herbal-industry waste converted into biomanure and remained material directly decanted into the environment and water canals. These materials pollute the natural water resources and indirectly affect the flora and fauna [21]. The synthesized GQDs have been utilized for the investigation of its fluorescence properties and selective detection of curcumin. Although a lot of work has been done on curcumin sensing but the effect of the pH and sensing of curcumin at different pH has not been explored entirely. Thus, it is necessary to establish an effective as well as a well-suited method for the rapid, simple, and cost-efficient detection of curcumin at low concentration. In the present study, we reported a facile approach of the conversion of bamboo timber waste biomass into CNCs following its green, one-pot transformation into GQDs. Moreover, we emphasized, the typical mechanism of conversion of cellulose nanocrystals into GQDs. Bamboo fiber-graphene quantum dots (Bf-GQDs) serve as a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based sensor for curcumin detection, established on its fluorescence quenching mechanism. The synthesized Bf-GQDs was successfully evaluated for selectivity with metal ions against curcumin.

Bamboo timber waste collected from the local sawmill from Shirpur (Dhule), India. Curcumin is generously gifted from Asoj Soft Caps Pvt. Ltd., Gujarat, India. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and other biochemicals were purchased from Rankem Pvt. Ltd. (Mumbai) India. Dialysis bags (12,000 kDa) were purchased from Himedia Laboratories (Mumbai), India. All chemicals and reagents used for the study were of analytical grades and used as received without further purification.

The bamboo timber waste having unevenly shaped strings was cut using the sharp cutter and milled using stone mortar and pestle to achieve a fine powder. The obtained powder then immersed in a 25% nitric acid aqueous solution and kept for 6 h with constant stirring. After, the solution of KClO3 at a 5 wt% ratio basis on the weight of nitric acid was added in the above solution. The resultant mixture was then kept for 24 h at 60 °C with occasional stirring. The obtained dispersion was cooled and washed by centrifugation (20,000 rpm) with DI water for multiple times to remove the possible reacted impurities. The accomplished slurry was then subjected to freeze-drying to achieve dry bamboo fiber powder (Bf) [22].

The cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) from bamboo timber fibers (Bf) were prepared using the reported method with some modifications [23]. In brief, 500 mg of the dried Bf was dispersed in 50 mL of 60% H2SO4 solution and the dispersion was heated to 70 °C on a hot plate with constant stirring for 30 min. Later, at 40 °C, 70 mL of ice-cold water was directly added in the beaker containing the dispersion which subsequently neutralized with 5 M NaOH solution and washed with DI water by centrifuge at 20,000 rpm. The obtained product was collected and subjected to the freeze-drying to obtain Bf-CNCs.

The synthesis of GQDs from the Bf-CNCs was achieved using the one-pot hydrothermal method. In brief, 200 mg of Bf-CNCs were dispersed in 20 mL of DI water and sonicated for 20 min. Then, the dispersion was transferred into a Teflon lined autoclave kept for 8 h at 180 °C. After completion of the reaction, the vessel was cooled at room temperature. The resultant dark-brown dispersion was subjected to ultrasonication for 10 min at room temperature get uniform dispersion. Afterword, the dispersion was filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to remove insoluble carbonaceous materials. The filtrate was kept for 24–36 h to rinse via a dialysis bag (12,000 kDa). The outer yellowish-brown solution of Bf-GQDs collected and subjected to freeze-drying. The obtained dry powder was used for further analysis.

The sensing of curcumin was carried out at different concentrations in an ethanol-water mixture. Firstly, FL spectra of Bf-GQDs was noted at the excitation wavelength of 410 nm and at a different concentration from 10 to 60 μL simultaneously. Further, interference of curcumin and potentialities of Bf-GQDs in different pH buffer solutions was also investigated. Initially, curcumin (10 μM) was added in the (5 μM/5 mL) solution of Bf-GQDs with fixed concentration and the fluorescence spectrum was noted. In the second step, curcumin with fixed concentration (10 μM) was added in an increasing concentration of Bf-GQDs (from 10 to 120 μM) in each in 10 mL of DI water. Lastly, curcumin (10 μM) was added in the 100 μM of Bf-GQDs solution with the same concentration at different pH buffer solutions for pH stability analysis. Quenching efficiency (%) of curcumin toward Bf-GQDs was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
%
Quenching
efficiency
(
I o
− I )
I
× 100
where, I
o is an initial intensity and I is the measured intensity of Bf-GQDs, respectively.

The investigation of real samples using carbon dots and nitrogen-doped GQDs with various fruit juice was reported earlier [24]. To investigate the potential in the practical application of the proposed strategy, the standard addition method was used with Ginger rhizomes juice (GJ). Ginger rhizome was collected from the local market, Shirpur. The rhizome washed properly to remove sand particles and chopped into small pieces and ground in a mortar-pestle to obtain the juice. The obtained juice was then filtered and centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 15 min. Then, the supernatant was collected, subsequently, 0.368 mg of curcumin was dissolved in 1 mL of the obtained GJ supernatant to obtain a stock with a concentration of 1 mM. The Bf-GQDs solution (10 mg/100 mL) and a stock solution of curcumin solution were mixed made up volume of 1 mL with DI water. The Bf-GQDs solution (100 μL) and 200 μL, 300 μL and 500 μL of 0.1 mM stock the curcumin solution were mixed made up volume of 1 mL with the ethanol-water mixture. Finally, the mixture was taken for the emission spectrum (λex = 410 nm) measurement.

Briefly, a stock solution of Bf-GQDs 10 mg/100 mL in DI water was prepared. From this stock solution, a series of dilutions were prepared, further 10 μL of the stock was sequentially added in the 10 mL of DI water as well as different pH-buffer solutions. The effect of pH was measured using the changes in the UV–visible spectra, fluorescence intensity, and zeta potential analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of synthesized Bf-CNCs and Bf-GQDs

ce:section.ce:para: Prepared Bf-CNCs and Bf-GQDs were analyzed by using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV 1800 Shimadzu, Japan). Absorption spectra of curcumin in the ethanol-water mixture were recorded in the range of 200–600 nm. The fluorescence spectra of the Bf-GQDs were recorded at a fixed and varying wavelength to measure the excitation dependent behavior in DI water. The effect of different pH solutions (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11) on the fluorescent intensity of Bf-GQDs was demonstrated.

The FT-IR spectrum was recorded for plain Bf-CNCs and Bf-GQD using an FT-IR spectrophotometer (IR-Affinity-1, Shimadzu, Japan). Raman analysis of Bf-GQDs was done using Horiba (HR800-UV), confocal micro-Raman spectrometer at room temperature with 532 nm laser at a power of 50 mW. Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS or EDX) used to provide qualitative and quantitative surface analysis. The X-ray photoelectron data acquired using Versa Probe III (Physical Electronics, IIT Roorkee, India) equipped with high performance 0–5 keV Ar+ ion gun and optional 10 and 20 kV C60 ion guns. PXRD of the synthesized Bf-CNCs and Bf-GQDs were recorded using Bruker D8 advance (STIC Cochin, India). The Crystallinity index of the CNCs was calculated by using equation (1) (Seagal method) [25];
(2)
I
c r y
−
I
a m r
I
c r y
× 100

The Bf-CNCs and Bf-GQDs were analyzed for particle size and zeta potential measurement using Dynamic Light Scattering using NanoPlus3 (Particulate System, Micromeritics, USA). Bf-GQDs were studied using field emission gun scanning electron microscope (FESEM – JEOL) 6390LA/Oxford) at accelerating voltage 20 kV. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) was performed on a JEOL/JEM2100 equipped with SAED facility operating at 200 kV. The sample was prepared by using an ultra-microtome (STIC Cochin, India).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization and discretion of synthesis methods
ce:section.ce:para: Bamboo timber waste powder as a raw material was chosen owing to its high content of cellulose (~74%) and hemicellulose (~26%). The high filament strength of bamboo fibers in contrast to other wood materials and valorization of waste biomass into active materials is the basis of choosing waste timber biomass. Owing to the high level of control possible over the size and PL of the GQDs high production yield a bottom-up approach (hydrothermal synthesis) was executed for Bf-GQDs synthesis [26]. The production yield of the Bf-GQDs calculated against the precursor concentration used and it was found to be 57 (wt. %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of GQDs formation from cellulose nanocrystals
ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose fibers (Bf) consist of crystalline regions and disordered (amorphous) regions. At the crystalline region, cellulose chains are restricted together by strong and highly convoluted intra- and inter-molecular hydrogen bonding. The amorphous realms are regularly distributed along the fibrils. The breakdown of these microfibrils results in a shorter crystalline part known as cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs) [27]. It is a generally accepted fact that the amorphous regions of cellulose are prone to hydrolysis than crystalline [28]. Authors assented that, the hydrothermal pre-treatment of Bf majorly losses the amorphous regions without substantial loss of the crystalline region. Initially, the process of nanocrystals formation involves recrystallization of cellulose and the post-hydrolysis affords the amorphous cellulose and subsequently glucose, which on separation afforded cellulose nanocrystals [29]. The catalytic conversion of CNCs into GQDs is a multistep process, but the hydrothermal treatment affords it as one-pot synthesis. The mechanism can be explained in a brief as; under high temperature and pressure (hydrothermal condition) water molecules split to produce a huge amount of H+ and OH− ions. Generally, cellulosic material classified into graphitizable and non-graphitizable carbons depending on the sources, phase compositions and structural types. It was evidenced that cellulose, especially crystalline cellulose with firm structure can yield cellulosic carbon which may extend the capability for graphitization. The cellulose found in the bamboo tree proved for its strength and tunability which is the main motive for discrimination as a precursor [30]. In the transformation of CNCs to carbon, the establishment of the graphitic structure takes place through the chain scission of cellulose molecules by the cleavage of 1.4-glycosidic linkages and succeeding polymerization by intramolecular redisposition, which ultimately leads to the foundation of a graphitic structure. The formation of a ladder-like structure (Fig. 4-e) during carbonization mostly begins with the formation of carbon-rich chains along the alignment of the core cellulose chains, preserving a facsimile of the native fibrillar structure. As a result, it is plausible that the high crystallinity of CNCs is advantageous to develop a highly ordered and firm graphitic structure [31,32]. It was also noticed that the high-temperature treatment of cellulosic materials results in depolymerization which leads to consequent transformation into the Levoglucosan (LG), Galactosan, and Mannosan as blackish-brown dispersion [30,33]. A schematic representation of Bf-GQDs formation from Bf-CNCs is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

The aqueous dispersion of the Bf-GQDs was subjected to UV light showed greenish fluorescence (at 254 nm) and strong blue fluorescence (at 365 nm) (Electronic Supplementary Material; Fig. S1). Interestingly, after dialysis (MW12,000 kDa), a drastic increase in the fluorescent intensity and hyperchromic shift in UV–vis was observed. Bf-GQDs after dialysis appeared as more pale-brown in natural light and uniformly colored in either wavelength 254 (pale greenish) and 365 (strong blue). The graph represented in the (Electronic Supplementary Material, Fig. S2). Showed the average diameter of Bf-CNCs has noted about 98.1 nm with zeta potential-29.33 mV indicating good stability. The presence of negative charge on the Bf-CNCs developed by the sulfate groups which might be added due to the sulfuric acid hydrolysis. In the case of Bf-GQDs, the average diameter of 9.8 nm and zeta potential about −16.65 mV was noted. The UV–vis spectrum of Bf-GQDs and curcumin were recorded. The Bf-GQDs exhibited a typical absorption band at 263.6 nm and 321.4 nm, corresponding to the π-π* and the n-π* transitions, respectively (Fig. 2 -B) [4].

The PL of Bf-GQDs varies with the nature of solvents and concentration whereas the size of Bf-GQDs directly affects the fluorescent intensity shift and illumination under UV light. Fluorescence spectrum of the Bf-GQDs in DI water shows excitation dependent emission with different concentrations also recorded at wavelength 410 nm in the range of 350 nm–650 nm (Fig. 2-d). Bf-GQDs showed maximum intensity at the excitation wavelength of 410 nm indicating high PL, the Bf-GQDs fluorescence was recorded at different concentrations (Fig. 2-f). The PL quantum yield of Bf-GQDs was calculated to be about 38.9%. (Electronic Supplementary Material).

The SEM image clearly indicates that Bf-GQDs appears to be coarse and in the dispersed form (Fig. 3 -c). The elemental analysis of Bf-GQDs revealed the existence of carbon 41.10 and oxygen 56.9 (wt.%) implying the transformation of Bf into the graphitic structure [34]. As compared to the Bf-CNCs (Fig. 3-b) the oxygen content in Bf-GQDs found to be relatively high. Bf-GQDs with high oxygen and hydroxyl functionalities can be evidenced by a steep peak in the EDX graph (Fig. 3-d). On the other hand, the carbon element entangled in the aromatic sp2 network indicates conversion into an independent element [35]. The FT-IR spectrum of Bf-CNCs exhibited the peak intensity in the range of 3600–3100 cm−1, 2900 cm−1, 1259 cm−1 and 898 cm−1 attributed due to the –OH stretching, C–H stretching, C–O stretching and C–O–C stretching for β-(1–4)-glycosidic linkages, respectively (Fig. 3-e). Similarly, the FT-IR spectrum of Bf-GQDs implies the presence of abundant hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and epoxy functionalities at 3403.57 cm−1, 1637.6 cm−1, 1443 cm−1, and 1179.14 cm−1, respectively [34]. The FE-SEM images of the Bf-CNCs exhibited the transformation of Bf-CNCs into smaller fibrillar structures arranged consecutively (Fig. 3-a).

The HRTEM images of Bf-CNCs elucidated the evenly arranged fibrillar structure (Fig. 4-a and b) which might be due to the over-irradiation of electron beams during HRTEM analysis. Bf-GQDs show even distribution and can be identified as spherical shaped crystal nature (Fig. 4-c, d, e). It reveals that prepared Bf-GQDs were uniform in size ranging from 2 to 8 nm in diameter and average particle size 2.8 ± 0.5 nm. The distances amid the lattice fringes were found to be 0.27 nm [10]. The selected area diffraction pattern (SAED), revealed the generation of spots, each spot corresponds to a gratified diffraction condition of the Bf-GQDs crystal structure (Fig. 4-f). The SAED of Bf-GQDs gives a ring pattern that confirms the crystalline nature of the Bf-GQDs [36].

The Bf-CNCs showed characteristic peaks at 2 θ = 19.0°, 22.62°, 31.9°, and 33.9° which are corresponding to various planes (Fig. 5 -a). The peak at 19.0° and 22.62° correspond to the plane (012) and (200) of the CNCs, whereas characteristic diffraction peaks at angle 22.62° and 33.9oanalogous to the cellulose crystal (200) and (004) planes, respectively. The Bf-GQDs afforded a typical graphitic peak in the range of 22.49° – 25.4° (002) which indicates the presence of a layered structure while broader nature specifies the smaller size of GQDs (Fig. 5-b) [37]. The d-value was around 2.00–2.81 which can be deduced in correlation with the HRTEM data (0.27 Å), which signifies the crystallinity of the material. Moreover, apparent peaks at 43.5 °and 31.74 with Miller indices (100), (102) confirm the well-formed crystal planes in cubic crystals fashion [38]. X-ray diffraction patterns showed the highly crystalline nature of Bf-CNCs and Bf-GQDs calculated using equation (2) which found to be 36.6% and 80.9%, respectively.

The ratio of sp3/sp2 carbon moiety one can be evidenced by using Raman shifts of the D and G bands. The graphene structure of Bf-GQDs was investigated by Raman spectra of Bf-GQDs revealed the characteristic (disordered structures of carbon) D-band (sp3) at 1352 cm−1 and crystalline G-band (sp2) implying graphitic nature by an accomplished peak at 1576 cm−1 (Fig. 5-c) [39].

The Raman spectra of synthesized Bf-GQDs represent the ID/IG ratio 0.78 suggests that the Bf-GQDs highly crystalline nature and consisting integrated graphite structure [40]. The existence of oxygen-containing functionalities in the Bf-GQDs structure has been evident by the FTIR spectrum which was supported by an XPS spectrum. The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the Bf-GQDs skeleton (Fig. 5-d). The survey spectrum shows the presence of O1s (531.6 eV) and of C1s (285.1 eV). High-resolution XPS spectra of elements, C1sspectra showed a peak at 285.1 eV indicate the presence C-sp2, C=O, and C–O–C of the carbon skeleton, carboxylic, and epoxy functionalities in the GQDs structure [2]. The O1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 531.8 and 532.8 eV associated with C=O, and C–OH bands, respectively (Fig. 5-e, f). The Raman, XRD, HR-TEM, and XPS reflect an insight into the formation of Bf-GQDs and the transformation of Bf-CNCs into a graphitic domain (GQDs).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH, particle size, and zeta potential on the fluorescent properties of Bf-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The intermolecular arrangement of these Bf-GQDs was due to π-π stacking, owing to this either overlapping or a head-to-tail arrangement causes a change in the absorptive and fluorescent properties [41]. Moreover, the experimental findings revealed that the particle size and zeta potential of the Bf-GQDs drastically affected by pH. An increase in the pH values affords as monotonic elevation in emission intensity of Bf-GQDs. High alkaline conditions result in aggregative behavior of Bf-GQDs whereas strong acidic environments relatively diminish the fluorescence properties of Bf-GQDs (Fig. S6 (Electronic Supplementary Material). While working with the fluorescence spectroscopy, a commonly observed problem is the ‘inner filter effect’ (IFE) which affects the spectral measurements. This might occur due to the overlap of excitation and emission spectra following the light emitted in the center and it can be reabsorbed by the sample itself because the ray cannot pass through the sample which results in surface fluorescence. This is the first report on the combined effect of concentration, particle size, and pH on the fluorescence spectrum and UV–vis spectrum of Bf-GQDs. The effect of particle size of the GQDs fluorescent characteristics has been demonstrated Fig. S4 (Electronic Supplementary Material).

Stability is the crucial factor need to examine before subjecting the Bf-GQDs for practical sensing applications. The influences of UV light on the fluorescence stability of Bf-GQDs has been evaluated by subjecting samples (10 mg L−1) at 365 nm. The fluorescence intensity of Bf-GQDs remained unchanged under UV irradiation even up to ~2 h. After, a substantial decrease in intensity was observed after 2 h. In our perspectives, this is the first attempt to assess the stability of GQDs for its UV-light tolerability. Generally, fluorescent dyes used to assess bioimaging at cellular levels, but are much susceptible to photobleaching. Regarding the futuristic use, the accomplished Bf-GQDs have been evaluated whether they prone to photobleaching or not. The findings conclude the excellent photobleaching tolerance of Bf-GQDs, consecutively a time-dependent and linear diminishing of FL intensity was noted (Fig. S5, Electronic Supplementary Material).

To ensure that, synthesized Bf-GQDs can be used for highly sensitive quantification of curcumin, the fluorescence responses were demonstrated in the ethanol-water mixture. Initially, a series of fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded at an emission wavelength (410 nm). Further, spectra were obtained upon the addition of curcumin ranging from 0 μM to 200 μM with an increase of 10. The fluorescence of Bf-GQDs solution decreased gradually with the increase of curcumin concentration. An excellent linear relationship was obtained between the quenching efficiency (F0 –F/F0) and the concentration of curcumin in the range from 3.0 to 1.5 μM (Fig. 6 - A). The fluorescence quenching was calculated from the equation; I0–I/I0 = 0.0051x+ 0.0177 with R2 = 0.9758, where I and I0 are the fluorescence intensities of Bf-GQDs at an excitation wavelength of 410 nm with and without curcumin, respectively. Further, the LOD of Bf-GQDs obtained was about 30.07 nM. The LOQ was calculated using a formula as previously reported literature (LOD = 3 σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of synthesized Bf-GQDs before the addition of curcumin, and S represents the slope of a calibration curve) and it was found to be 1.0 μM [42]. Quenching efficiency (%) of curcumin toward Bf-GQDs was calculated using equation (2) and it was found to be 77.83.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of Bf-GQDs quenching by curcumin
ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin exists in two tautomeric forms namely keto-enol tautomer's [43]. A substantial solvent-dependent shift in the absorption maxima of curcumin was observed as the solvent polarity and hydrogen bond-donating or accepting ability changes. The large solvatochromic red-shift can be noted in a polar (protic) solvent like methanol due to the effect of both polarity and or hydrogen bonding ability of the solvent on the excited state of curcumin [44]. The enol form possesses good stability in non-polar and slightly polar solvents, due to the protracted conjugation along with the p-electron. Curcumin is a weak Brønsted acid exhibiting three labile protons with different prototropic equilibrium. Although enolic -OH of curcumin is much acidic with actively participates in the ionic interaction with surface functionalities of Bf-GQDs. This phenomenon helps to enhance the solubility of curcumin in aqueous solvents forms is the basic foundation of our quenching mechanism in the ethanol-water mixture. Generally, emission spectra of Bf-GQDs showed a quite precise overlapping with the absorption band of curcumin, due to which curcumin could absorb or trap the emission light from Bf-GQDs (Fig. 6- B). The spectral overlapping suggested that the fluorescence change might be related to the energy transfer (FRET), or electron transfer [42]. More precisely, the probable clarification for the strong quenching Bf-GQDs by Curcumin could be the FRET, where the Bf-GQDs are energy donors and Cur is the energy acceptor Fig. 6-C. The fluorescence quenching of GQDs by curcumin demonstrated by the Stern-Volmer equation (Fig. S3, Electronic Supplementary Material).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of curcumin sensing in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the practical application of the proposed approach, different concentrations of Cur (100, 300, and 500 mM) are spiked GJ supernatant. The result shows good accuracy in relation to our proposed method. The method with the high recovery in the range of 98.9%–104.2% for GJ depicted in Table 1 . The coefficients of variation for the quantification are found to be in the range of 0.35–0.73%.

To estimate the selectivity of the proposed method, the effect of some metal ions (Al3+, Ca2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, K+, Na+) and other biochemicals (urea, lactose, fructose, glucosamine and citric acid were investigated on Bf-GQDs against detection of curcumin. The fluorescent intensity of the Bf-GQDs did not affect upon addition of metal ions aliquots. The results accomplished that the Bf-GQDs probe showed an outstanding selectivity toward the sensing of curcumin. After the addition of 10 μL of curcumin, in the same concentration of the Bf-GQDs sample, a drastic decrease in the fluorescent intensity was observed (Fig. 7 ). The results evidenced the remarkable selectivity of Bf-GQDs toward sensing of curcumin in the aqueous environment.
Our investigational findings have been reported with the few recent kinds of literature elucidating the detection of curcumin (Table 2 ).

A facile and eco-friendly approach for the efficient conversion of bamboo timber waste into CNCs with simple acid hydrolysis has been developed. The accomplished CNCs further transformed into fluorescent probes (Bf-GQDs) by the one-pot hydrothermal method. Fabricated Bf-GQDs further demonstrated for the highly sensitive and selective quantitation for curcumin using the FRET effect in protic solvents. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching mechanism due to the surface protonation and FRET effect of the curcumin on the fluorescence intensity of Bf-GQDs has been revealed. The quenching kinetics of Bf-GQDs by photo-physical intermolecular interaction have been demonstrated using the Stern-Volmer plot. Similarly, the anomalous behavior of Bf-GQDs in the aqueous system with different pH values and particle size also demonstrated. We believed that this method might hearten a new way for further exploration and practical applications for curcumin sensing and efficient recognition of materials.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), Department of Science and Technology, Government of India (ECR/2017/000905), and H. R. Patel Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research, Shirpur, for providing necessary facilities. We are gratified to IIT, Roorkee for providing XPS analysis facilities and STIC, Cochin for the essential characterization facility.

ce:section.ce:para:
